output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.7, "R1.2": 0.8, "R1.3": 0.9, "R1.4": 0.9, "R1.5": 0.5, "R1.6": 0.6, "R1.7": 0.7}}

Critique of the introduction/abstract section. The core problems are an unusually narrow baseline comparison, overclaimed novelty, and a few softer issues in evidence reporting and framing.

**Baseline is too narrow to support the headline claim.** The entire empirical case rests on CalibVar vs. CalibVar-v1 — the authors' own prior model. Missense pathogenicity is a crowded field: AlphaMissense, ESM1b/ESM-variant scoring, PrimateAI-3D, REVEL, VARITY, EVE, CADD, and MutPred2 are standard reference points, and several report calibration analyses. Without at least a subset of these baselines (or an explicit, defended reason for omitting them — e.g., parameter-matched constraint), the AUROC numbers (0.943 / 0.901 / 0.872) cannot be interpreted. A reader has no way to know whether CalibVar is state-of-the-art, mid-pack, or behind. The phrase "substantial step forward for uncertainty-aware variant interpretation" is not supported by what is reported.

**Novelty claim is overstated and oddly hedged.** "We are unaware of any prior method that achieves comparable calibration on the ClinVar-2024 split at the same parameter budget" is doing a lot of work with three qualifiers (same split, same parameter budget, "unaware of"). Temperature scaling (Guo et al., 2017) and deep ensembles (Lakshminarayanan et al., 2017) are both standard; their combination is not novel in the ML literature. The novelty is presumably the application to variant pathogenicity with a specific training/calibration recipe — say that directly, and cite the methods you are composing. As written, the sentence reads as a defensively narrow superlative.

**Evidence-claim gaps.**
- The 92.4% vs 84.1% high-confidence comparison: on which benchmark? Averaged? At what sample count? An 8-point gap is meaningless without n and a confidence interval — high-confidence subsets can be small.
- ECE "averaged across the three benchmarks" hides per-benchmark numbers. Calibration is benchmark-specific (label noise, prior shift); the per-benchmark ECE should be in the table, not just the mean.
- No confidence intervals or significance tests anywhere. With three benchmarks and modest deltas (e.g., 0.872 vs 0.859 on BRCA-Exchange — a 0.013 AUROC gap), this matters.
- "Reliability diagrams (Section 4) further confirm…" — fine as a forward reference, but the abstract/intro should not lean on a figure the reader cannot see.

**Train/test contamination is not addressed.** ClinVar-2024 held-out, gnomAD, and BRCA-Exchange overlap heavily in variant content. If CalibVar-v1 (2023) and CalibVar were trained on overlapping ClinVar snapshots, a 2024 "held-out" split may still include variants seen during training of upstream feature extractors (ESM, AlphaFold-derived features, etc.). Standard practice now is to report a label-date-stratified evaluation or at minimum to state the deduplication procedure. This is a routine reviewer objection in this subfield and should be preempted.

**Framing problems.**
- "Point pathogenicity scores without confidence signals" is inaccurate for prior work — REVEL, AlphaMissense, and EVE all emit a continuous score that downstream tools threshold; some (e.g., EVE) explicitly report uncertainty. The contrast should be calibration quality, not the existence of a score.
- "Straightforward to integrate into ACMG-based curation pipelines" is asserted but not demonstrated. ACMG/AMP integration involves mapping a probability to evidence strength (PP3/BP4 tiers per Pejaver et al., 2022) — non-trivial and currently contested. Either show the mapping or drop the claim.
- "Fewer than 200 lines of code" is an odd thing to foreground in an abstract; it doesn't signal scientific contribution and reads as filler.

**Structural suggestion.** The fourth paragraph compresses three different claims (novelty, integration ease, broad utility) into one. Split: contribution statement first, then a concrete integration scenario (with the ACMG mapping shown or cited), then forward references to the rest of the paper. The current ordering puts the strongest weakness (unsupported novelty claim) before the weaker but more salient practical contribution.

**Prose-level.**
- "Anonymous Authors et al., 2023" — fix before submission; if this is for double-blind review, cite as "(prior work, citation withheld)" or similar per venue convention.
- "Among predictions with model confidence above 0.9, CalibVar is correct 92.4% of the time" — "correct" is ambiguous for a binary-with-threshold task; specify (accuracy at argmax? Brier? per-class recall?).
- "Closes the gap between predicted and empirical accuracy across the full probability range" — quantify or remove; "closes the gap" without a number is a claim, not evidence.

**Recommended minimum additions before this section is defensible:** (1) at least three external baselines on at least one shared benchmark, (2) per-benchmark ECE and reliability numbers with CIs, (3) an explicit statement on train/test overlap and deduplication, (4) replacement of the novelty sentence with a precise contribution claim that names the composed methods.

Uncertainty: I have not verified that AlphaMissense, PrimateAI-3D, EVE, etc. all report on ClinVar-2024 specifically — some may use earlier ClinVar snapshots, which is exactly the contamination point above. The Pejaver et al. 2022 reference to ACMG PP3/BP4 calibration is from memory and should be checked. The claim that EVE reports uncertainty is based on its use of a VAE-derived evidence score; whether that constitutes "calibrated uncertainty" in the sense the authors mean is debatable and worth checking before citing.
